title: About
text:
Who I Am
I’m Han (Simon) Yan, a data scientist and quantitative researcher currently pursuing a Master of Professional Studies in Information Science at Cornell University, with a focus on AI Assurance Technology.
My work sits at an unusual intersection: I care deeply about making AI systems not just smart, but trustworthy — reliable enough that a business can stake real decisions on them. That means thinking carefully about data integrity, model transparency, and the gap between a model’s performance on paper and its behavior in the wild.
Before Cornell, I worked at Central China Securities, where I built data pipelines handling hundreds of thousands of financial records daily and developed multi-factor alpha signals for equity research. That experience taught me that the unglamorous work — cleaning, validating, and stress-testing data — is often where real analytical value is created.

title: About
text:
Academic Foundation
My quantitative backbone comes from deep study of:

Stochastic Processes & Markov Chains — modeling systems that evolve probabilistically over time
Monte Carlo Simulation — quantifying uncertainty where closed-form solutions don’t exist
Machine Learning — with a critical eye toward when and why models fail
Geospatial Analysis — visualizing socioeconomic phenomena across space and time

I believe mathematical rigor and practical engineering need each other. A model that can’t be deployed is a thought experiment. A pipeline without statistical foundations is a liability.

title: About
text:
Beyond the Work
When I’m not in the weeds of a dataset, I’m interested in the broader questions of how quantitative tools shape decisions — in finance, in policy, and increasingly in AI governance. I’m based in Ithaca, NY, and I enjoy long walks by Cayuga Lake when the Ithaca weather cooperates (which is not always).

Why Data Assurance Is the Unglamorous Core of AI

2 min


AI Assurance

Data Engineering

Opinion



The gap between a model that works in a notebook and one that works in production is almost always a data problem — not a modeling problem.



Mar 1, 2025

title: Why Data Assurance Is the Unglamorous Core of AI
text:
Everyone wants to build the model. Nobody wants to validate the pipeline that feeds it.
This is the central tension I’ve observed across both academic ML courses and real-world financial data work: we spend enormous energy on architecture choices, hyperparameter tuning, and benchmark performance — and comparatively little on the question of whether the data coming in is actually trustworthy.
At Central China Securities, I built ETL pipelines processing 100K+ financial records daily. The most valuable thing I learned wasn’t pandas syntax or SQL optimization. It was this: the cost of a bad data point isn’t just a slightly worse model — it’s a compounding error that can silently corrupt every downstream signal you produce.

title: Why Data Assurance Is the Unglamorous Core of AI
text:
Everyone wants to build the model. Nobody wants to validate the pipeline that feeds it.
This is the central tension I’ve observed across both academic ML courses and real-world financial data work: we spend enormous energy on architecture choices, hyperparameter tuning, and benchmark performance — and comparatively little on the question of whether the data coming in is actually trustworthy.
At Central China Securities, I built ETL pipelines processing 100K+ financial records daily. The most valuable thing I learned wasn’t pandas syntax or SQL optimization. It was this: the cost of a bad data point isn’t just a slightly worse model — it’s a compounding error that can silently corrupt every downstream signal you produce.

title: Why Data Assurance Is the Unglamorous Core of AI
text:
What “Assurance” Actually Means
AI Assurance isn’t a product category or a compliance checkbox. It’s a design philosophy with three components:
1. Reliability — Does the system produce consistent outputs when given consistent inputs? Can you reproduce results?
2. Transparency — Can you explain why the model produced a given output? Not just in theory, but to a non-technical stakeholder who needs to act on it?
3. Robustness — Does the system degrade gracefully when data drifts, when edge cases appear, or when upstream sources change format without warning?
Most ML projects nail none of these. A few nail reliability. Almost none nail all three.

title: Why Data Assurance Is the Unglamorous Core of AI
text:
The Markov Chain Analogy
I find it useful to think about data pipelines as Markov chains. Each transformation step has a probability of introducing an error — a dropped row, a miscoded category, a join that silently drops nulls. These errors compound. If each of five steps has a 98% chance of being correct, your end-to-end correctness is only 0.98⁵ ≈ 90% — and that’s before you’ve run a single model.
Assurance engineering is the discipline of pushing each step’s reliability toward 99.9%+ through logging, schema validation, automated tests, and anomaly detection. It’s boring. It’s also what separates a research demo from a production system.

title: Why Data Assurance Is the Unglamorous Core of AI
text:
Where I’m Taking This
My current work at Cornell is exploring how to make assurance practices systematic — not something you bolt on after the model is built, but something baked into the pipeline from the start. More on that as the project develops.
If this resonates with you, I’d love to hear how you approach data reliability in your own work. Reach out via the Contact page.
